summary: Glucose is an aldohexose and fructose is a ketohexose; both share the molecular formula C6H12O6.
Glucose is a ketohexose and fructose is an aldohexose.
Glucose and fructose have different molecular formulae.
Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm and yields a net gain of 2 ATP per glucose molecule.
Glycolysis occurs in the mitochondria and yields 36 ATP per glucose molecule.
Glycolysis occurs in the nucleus and yields 4 ATP per glucose molecule.
Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1) is the rate-limiting enzyme of glycolysis.
Hexokinase is the rate-limiting enzyme of glycolysis.
Pyruvate kinase is the rate-limiting enzyme of glycolysis.
Lactose intolerance is caused by lactase deficiency, leading to bacterial fermentation of undigested lactose in the colon.
Lactose intolerance is caused by sucrase deficiency.
Lactose intolerance is an autoimmune condition that destroys intestinal villi.
HbA1c (glycated haemoglobin) reflects average blood glucose over 2-3 months and monitors diabetic control.
HbA1c reflects blood glucose levels over the past 24 hours.
HbA1c is measured only in the urine and reflects renal glucose handling.
The rate-limiting enzyme of gluconeogenesis is fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase.
The rate-limiting enzyme of gluconeogenesis is glucose-6-phosphatase.
Gluconeogenesis uses exactly the same enzymes as glycolysis in reverse.
The pentose phosphate pathway generates NADPH for biosynthetic reactions and ribose-5-phosphate for nucleotide synthesis.
The pentose phosphate pathway is the main pathway for ATP generation.
The pentose phosphate pathway occurs only in muscle cells during exercise.
